speaker: interviewer
text: Mark Grinims is being interviewed by Abby Ellin in English. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me recapitulate again. We are starting from the first action, that they took all the Jews out. They killed the old and the religious and the sick ones. The second action, they took all the young people out, everybody out. And I told them that I'm a carpenter, and they let me go. So did they let go other people, too, which was among close to 3,000 people. The third one was the ghetto. They surrounded, and I escaped from the ghetto with Dorothy. We went through the-- a friend, [NON-ENGLISH]. Her son was the Ukrainian policeman. We stayed there for a night or so, then we went to my friend in a subbasement, living there for about three months. And after that, he couldn't hold us longer, because somebody squealed. So he was afraid to keep us there. And then we're back to-- I went for a one night to my-- to the farmer, who didn't want to hold us because of whatever. Then we went back to [NON-ENGLISH]. We stayed there for a while, for about three to four months. Then she couldn't hold me there. She only could hold Dorothy for a while. And then she couldn't hold her, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went out looking for different places. I met a friend of mine, surprisingly enough, during the night. And we went to her house. And through the window we took Dorothy out, and we went to the woods. Were you afraid at this time? You kept on. You were going here, you were going there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Adrenaline high.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the only way? You just didn't even think?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't think, no. You work-- you do it by instinct. You don't think of the consequences. It's a survival instinct.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was just, I'm going to make it. I'm going to live through this.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't know if you're going to make it or not. But since it's an instinctive thing, you just do it, hoping for the best but not knowing what the outcome is going to be. And then we went to the woods, where we joined a group of 20, 30 people from our hometown. And we stayed there until-- you know the story about her being wounded?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me it from-- tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we were there in the woods. And then, suddenly, we heard shots. And she was shot in the leg. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was maybe 10, 15 feet away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you saw it happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I didn't see it happening, because she was maybe 15 feet away. I didn't know what happened, because everybody was running in different directions. If we would have stayed together, they would have either caught us together or shot us together. So everybody scattered as much as you could.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had just come into the woods in the middle of the night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was early, late afternoon. And they start shooting with machine guns. And she was-- one of our friends got killed. One was wounded. Then she was wounded, and yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other question. When you were in the woods, how long were you there, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were just lying out under the stars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under the stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have a hut. You didn't have a tent. You didn't have anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing under stars. If it was raining, you were sleeping on the wet grass. If it's not raining, you slept on the dry grass.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have blankets?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just whatever the coats. And that's it. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: And, during the day, did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the day, we tried not to go out any place. We just sat in one place in the woods. And at night, we went out and tried to get some bread or some potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the nearby peasants. And after that, when they start shooting, everybody spread out. And everybody ran out, nobody knew where. And that time, they took Dorothy. They carried her and then put her in the wagon to this doctor who couldn't do anything, because at that time, they didn't have any penicillin. They didn't have any antibiotics, nothing. They took her to the hospital, and you know rest of the story. She was in the hospital. They were supposed to amputate her leg, or at least the ankle, or above. And either due to neglect, or luck, whatever, she survived. And I used to visit her. Bringing her food, especially horse salami, which the Germans made very good. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Horse salami?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horse salami. Salami made of horses. And I used to bring her that or chicken, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that taste like? Horse--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like salami. And that's what happened. And after a while, when she left the hospital, the Germans start moving away. Somebody told them-- when we had this little apartment, for about, I will say, a month-- they told her that-- they told them that I am German or working with the Germans or for the Germans. And the Soviets came in, with two soldiers with machine guns. They arrested me. They took me for an interrogation. Dorothy, on crutches, came in at night. And to our luck, the interrogator was a Jewish major who understood our situation, and gave us a soldier with a wagon, and brought us home to Ostrog. This is the saga of Ostrog. Now, we came back. I mean, nobody wanted to give us anything, even though they stole from the Jews. Or maybe my aunt, or somebody else gave them some stuff to hold, to keep for after the war. They said that they never took it, never got it. And a week later, they took me to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like, going back to Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Ostrog? Well, first of all, it was just about two, three days. When we came in, was a very empty feeling, very strange feeling, because everything looked different. There was nobody there from our friends. And the people who were left there looked very suspiciously at us, not knowing what to expect. We were still afraid, because at that time they still had Ukrainian underground Bandera groups.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were afraid they could kill us. So we didn't know why we came so soon, but we had no place to go. So she stayed with Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH], the one that helped us for a couple of months to stay there, that her son was the policeman. And they took me into the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now before that, I forgot to mention the name of my friend, who kept going back and forth when she was in the hospital-- Wierzbicki, Csesek Wierzbicki. He's now in Florida. He came to visit her. He came to help me to change some gold coins for paper money. And he was in touch with me all the time. That's the one who gave me the contact of his cousins in, in that Slavuta place, where the general was overseeing that place. And after that, I lost contact with my other friend from Poland, Zygmunt Sinawski. And I just established contact with him about seven, eight years ago. And then, going back, we came to Ostrog. A week later, they took me to the army. Took me to the army, they sent me to Siberia, to Chkalovsk-- which is 60 below zero-- for basic training. So it's 60 below zero--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia, deep Russia, the uranium mountains. So I was there for basic training, for six weeks. They had to give us a-- first like an officer school type of a thin. And then send me after-- yeah, I got some of hepatitis there, from the food or from whatever. And I stayed with their hospital for about two weeks. After that, they sent us to the front. They sent us to Szczecin, which is in Poland, closer to the German border. And we were fighting for a week, street fights. And how I came alive, I don't know. If you see it on television, how they do it, so you'll know how they do street fighting, and that's how we were fighting. We took over Szczecin. And then we supervised the town, looking for some liquor and looking for some subversive or looking for whatever. And after that, we went a few miles past Szczecin, staying there. Staying, and I tried to get a pass to go to Wroclaw, which is a town in Poland, and on the way there, to stop in Lodz where Dorothy was. When I came to Lodz, some of my friends from before the war, who escaped to Russia-- and they were active in the Zionist organization before the war, and they were active now as Aliyah Bet to help Jews to escape to Israel-- they told me take the uniform. Get rid of the gun, get rid of everything. Put some civilian clothes and we'll ship you to Austria or Czechoslovakia and Germany as a displaced person. And they were looking for her, because she was a translator with all kinds of secret information. And they were looking for me, because I actually escaped. Now this was right after the war. The war ended in May 15, and this was probably, like, in June. And so from there, we went to Czechoslovakia, then Austria, through the borders during the night as refugees or displaced people. And we went to Germany, to the camp of Foehrenwald. We stayed there for a month. Then we went to Munich. Then I worked in the Jewish committee, and I was chairman of the historical committee. I was going to medical school. And the reason I should have stayed there longer, because most of my friends finished medical school in Germany. When they came here, they were already more or less established. But being my brother was a doctor here, my sister was a doctor. Her uncle was supposed to be rich. I figure that probably, when I come here, I will have no problem getting into school. Unfortunately, I couldn't get into school so easily. And I was advised by my friends, who finished school there, go for anything-- I don't care-- any kind of, of healing field. And through somebody that I knew, recommended chiropractor. I figured, I'll try it for a year and see how I like it. And that's how I got involved, and I finished school here. And this was the story. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Very easy. Not-- have you been back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went back to Poland, to see my friend Zygmunt Sinawski, the one who put me in the cellar for two, three nights in the theater in November, when I went to get my sister back to Poland. And I saw him there. That's the first time I was in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was never in Warsaw before, so I don't know. I just strictly went to see him. I felt some kind of obligation to visit him, and I felt good about it. So I went to see him. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You haven't been back to Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, some friends went there for a dedication ceremony, which occurred a couple months ago. I didn't go. But 70 or 80 people supposedly went there, to dedicate the stone for the-- in the place where they'd killed the [INAUDIBLE], I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you practice any kind of Judaism now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I just go to temples and high holy days, and all important holy days. Otherwise, I don't practice, actively, any religious Judaism.

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked your wife the same question--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she [INAUDIBLE] no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --I don't know if you heard earlier-- now whether she believes in God, whether you believe in God.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you elaborate on that? I mean, did you always? Consistently, through the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war, I never thought about it, because it became-- it's and esoteric type of a question, that you can't say if you believe in nature or the Holy Spirit or God or some kind of universal intelligence. I mean, it's all probably means the same. So if somebody will say, I don't believe in any universal intelligence-- call it God, call it High Spirit-- then it's different. But I do believe in it. I don't believe that He knows what's going on every minute of my life, or anybody else's life. But somebody created the universes, and somebody put order in that. So I believe that there must be some force that conducts all those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And well, do you-- why do you think that you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I think I survived? My wife thinks, all the time, that it's luck. There is always the element of luck involved. But if you just sit-- like some of my friends and other people who are very intelligent people, they sit hoping either God will help them, or something will help them. Or they'll drop a bomb, and everybody will disappear. I didn't believe in that, so I feel that luck is an important element, if you do something about it to be in the right time and the right place. That's why you have to take papers. You have to have-- like in my case, I had these Polish papers-- establish some kind of communication with people who can help you in the future. Get some money-- you know what I mean-- so you can put your way through in, in critical situations. That, to me, is important. Luck, maybe, comes in someplace in this, into that picture. But I would not say that everything is luck or coincidence, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well there's-- I mean, there's a certain amount of cunning on your part. I mean, you got the papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, and escaping here and cultivated friendship with policemen and cultivated friendship with some people all over, just in case something. I wouldn't call it luck. I mean that cultivation means it's planning, planning. Now, after you plan everything, and if you're lucky, your planning works out. But if you-- if you're lucky, if you don't plan anything, you'll be hanged by the luck. So that my definition of luck in my case.

speaker: interviewer
text: I still want to know about the German soldiers. Did you feel like you were betraying your Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't do anything. They didn't know who I. I wasn't Mark Grinims, I was Skrbacki. And I was hiding. And I was justified to do anything, just to save my life. I mean, I didn't do any-- I didn't fight. I didn't do anything. I was just sitting like an idiot, watching the prisoners cutting down trees in the woods. I mean, it was just looking at them. They shouldn't escape, whatever. But I didn't do anything actively, or I was in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you have done if one of them had tried to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never thought about it. I would never thought about it to begin with. I don't think they would even try to escape, because they needed some food. So this wasn't anything that came into my mind at that time. At that time, I had to play the role, and try to, to make everything believable as it is, so I can survive and Dorothy could survive. Without me, she couldn't survive at that time, because she needed me in the hospital, and after the hospital. So I had to maintain all that image, and do the best I can, just to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I don't even know how you met, you and Dorothy.

speaker: interviewee
text: We know each other since childhood-- I mean, from teenage years, from the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: At school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, more or less. You mingle the same. We belonged to the same Betar organization. We mingled, more or less, with the same people around. The radius of walking around with the opposite sex at that time was in a radius of maybe a quarter of a mile. So everybody was walking around here and there. You know? So everybody knew each other. And then during the war, somehow we got together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any point during the war when you thought that she was dead? When you were split up?

speaker: interviewee
text: At one point I thought so, when, when they-- when she was shot in the leg and she disappeared. And I thought maybe, if I never find her. But I was looking for her all night long. And I found her. An interesting point that comes to mind now-- the people who helped her at that time. When I was still living by the German-- at my home, after the first action, while I'm still in my house-- the furniture was there, everything was there. They didn't do anything yet. They just killed the older people. In between there, one of the-- one soldier who escaped the German war prison-- he was a boyfriend of one of the girls who stayed in our house during the Soviet occupation. And she was a roomer. And she escaped to Russia with the army. And he came from the German war prison. He escaped to my house. And I kept him there for two, three nights. And I never thought about it, that some day-- in a year, or two years-- that Dorothy will be shot in the woods. And somebody from his family will, by a coincidence, help her that night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing. That's really amazing. What else did I miss?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you the whole thing sounds a little bit fantastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel like it happened to you, or does it feel like it was somebody completely different?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] It happened to me. It happened to me. I remember the dates, I think. I think I remembered all the dates. I gave you the correct dates.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't feel like it was a separate person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I felt-- I told you, at that time, I felt like I didn't know where I am, because we had no contact with civilization, so to say, quote unquote. And we didn't know where we are. We didn't know what the outcome is going to be. So you felt a little subhuman, looking-- knowing that everybody lives a normal life. And you are, from minute to minute, can be squashed like, like a, you know, like a bug or killed by somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the time that you felt-- I can't even explain. I don't know if I can find the right term or right word for it. Felt on a subhuman level. You know what I mean? That's the only thing I could feel. It was very-- everything was very unrealistic. You know what I mean? The whole situation at that time-- since the Germans, maybe a month after the Germans came in, until the end of it-- everything to me sounded very unrealistic. Not real. When you were living in the cellar, just the two of you, and all you were getting was the potatoes, the mashed potatoes--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, kluski--

speaker: interviewer
text: For the three different meals. You just-- you did nothing all day, you just sat there and waited. You couldn't read. You couldn't write. You did nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was dark. You couldn't see anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't manage. You, you exist. Remember, I told you that you exist on a different level. And this was actually the existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's amazing. This tape is for your daughter, and your granddaughter, and future Grinims generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: For posterity in a time capsule.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, so is there anything you want to tell them? Anything you want to add? Anything you want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the only thing, I hope that none of them will go through what I went through, the rest of their lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have nightmares or flashbacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Because-- occasionally, I do. Occasionally, I do. But I just try to erase that unpleasant part of it. And try to make peace with that situation in my mind, so to say. I divide it into reality and unreality. And since I told you, that part that we lived, that was unreality. So I'm living reality.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you talked about your experiences before? Did you tell your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. She knows, more or less, that we survived the Holocaust, more or less, in the general terms, you know. But she doesn't know the details or anything like that. I told her, maybe, I was in the army, and I was fighting street fights, and all that stuff. But nothing about what-- the details we went through, the actions, the killings, and what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the story of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the house in Ostrog. [POLISH] 55, where I was born. It was taken two, three years ago by somebody who visited the town. It has very slight resemblance to the house that I have lived to, because it had a lot of different ornaments, and they look nice and clean. It had a little garden the front. But this is basically the house I have lived in, and I was born in. This is my father, Wolfe Grinims, who died in 1935. He had a very positive, good influence over me as a child, and as an adolescent. And when he died, even though I was only 13 years old, I missed him. And I think of him-- even today-- very fondly. This is a montage of a few pictures put together, which I took out from an album, which I couldn't separate. On my left is a picture of my sister, which perished in approximately the second action in 1941, in Rowno. On my right is my stepmother, who perished in Ostrog in the second action in 1941. On the lower right is a not too clear picture of me being in the Army, in Szczecin. In Poland, during the Second World War. And on the left is me being a teenager at the age of 16. On the bottom is the picture of my sister, from the top left, and her husband. My brother-in-law, who was killed by the Soviets because of being an active leader of the Zionist movement. And two of her friends, who were emigrated before the war to Israel. The picture here, is on the left side is a picture of a friend of mine, Csesek Wierzbicki, who is presently in Florida. He's the one who helped me to get a job with the German toy organization while Dorothy was in hospital. He's the one who helped me to arrange certain things while I was in Ostrog, and we still maintain active communication. He helped me tremendously during the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The picture on the upper left and lower left is me with my friend, Zygmunt Sinawski. That's the gentleman who helped me during the war in 1941, in Rowno. When I went to get my sister to bring back to Ostrog, he kept me for two or three days in the basement of a theater. And this picture is when we visit him in Poland, several years ago in Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Last picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: One more. I don't think-- well, the left picture is self-explanatory. That's me in diapers. The right one is my brother Henry, the one who studied in Austria and France, and came to the United States, luckily, right before the war. He practiced medicine here. The lower middle is part of my indirect family. Some uncles and aunts from Ostrog. They all perished in the second action in 1941, in Ostrog. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: May I introduce my beautiful daughter, Lorine. My beautiful granddaughter, Simone. My wife you met already before. You know me already from my stories that I-- what did I do with the stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: You told a story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them before, in detail, so I would not repeat myself. And my wife, I think, wants to say a few words about the dolls that she wanted, which we bought in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love my family dearly. My daughter and granddaughter. It's all we have left, now. I have nieces in California and New Jersey. The doll that I'm holding, I bought in Poland when we went to visit our dear friend Zygmunt. The reason I bought the two dolls is because the costumes remind me of the dance I did. It was the night of Polish dance that I did on the stage. And that's the exact costume that I wore.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how you met your husband, yes? He was playing the piano?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he played a piano, and I danced with a group of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to say something, Lorine or Simone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm the proud mother of Simone. And daughter of Dorothy and Mark. And it's really an honor to be part of this video and whole experience. I really can't relate to anything that they experienced in the war, because I was born in this country. And privileged to be born free, and without the prejudices and the plights that they had to undergo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as an American citizen, as a free American citizen, I'm very fortunate to be in a country of all different nationalities and religions and creeds. My daughter also is fortunate to be born here. But there's much to be learned from our history, so we don't repeat the same mistakes that others have made. Simone?

speaker: interviewee
text: is Simone, and I call my grandparents Boba and Papa. Sort of a nickname for grandparents. And I love them very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you all for being here.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's very nice. Beautiful. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
